title: OpenAI model disproves an 80-year-old Erdős geometry conjecture
subtitle: A general-purpose reasoning model independently overturned a 1946 claim in discrete geometry, and a Fields medalist checked the proof.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI announced on May 20 that one of its general-purpose reasoning models had autonomously disproved a conjecture in discrete geometry that Paul Erdős posed in 1946, concerning how points packed in the plane can be arranged at equal distances. The model was handed only the problem statement — no partial proof, no step-by-step human guidance — and produced a complete construction that beats the grid-like arrangement long assumed to be essentially optimal. OpenAI says this is the first time one of its models has independently solved a prominent open problem at the center of a mathematical subfield.

What makes the result striking is the route the model took. Rather than brute-forcing examples, it reached into deep algebraic number theory — Golod–Shafarevich theory and infinite class field towers — to build an infinite family of point configurations achieving a polynomial improvement over the square grid. That is a leap across mathematical domains, the kind of cross-field move that usually marks a strong human researcher rather than a search procedure.

External mathematicians checked the work and wrote a companion paper. Fields medalist Tim Gowers called it "a milestone in AI mathematics," Princeton combinatorialist Noga Alon called it "an outstanding achievement," and number theorist Arul Shankar said current models "go beyond just helpers to human mathematicians — they are capable of having original ingenious ideas." The caveats are real: the proof has not yet cleared journal peer review, and an earlier OpenAI math claim this year was walked back. But the arc over the past twelve months — from AI as proof-assistant to AI as conjecture-breaker — is steep.

Takeaway for learners: if you study math or computer science, the skill that is appreciating is not calculation — the machine has that — it is judgment. The mathematicians who validated this result added value precisely by verifying it, contextualizing it, and confirming the cross-field connection was sound. Train to be the person who can tell whether the machine's answer is actually correct and why it matters, because that is the part the model still cannot certify about its own work.
